results.title: New Way Now: Estée Lauder and Jo Malone London give scent discovery a spritz of AI with Google Cloud
highlights:
*Featured in this video:* Chris Curro, Principal Machine Learning Engineer at The Estée Lauder Companies
[...]
*Executive summary:* The Estée Lauder Companies and Jo Malone London partnered with Google Cloud to digitally recreate the iconic fragrance house’s in-store consultation experience. The Jo Malone AI Scent Advisor, trained on its deep scent expertise, lets customers describe what they’re looking for in their own words, using Google’s Gemini models and Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform (formerly Vertex AI) to translate their responses into expertly-tailored scent recommendations. The new tool also relies on Google Cloud — including Cloud Run, Pub/Sub, Firestore, BigQuery, and Model Armor — to ensure reliable, secure AI experiences at scale. Now, online shoppers can confidently discover their perfect scent in a way that feels personal, playful, and distinctly Jo Malone London, driving conversion, revenue, and overall customer satisfaction.
[...]
*Solution:* Partnering with Google Cloud, The Estée Lauder Companies’ AI and consumer tech teams developed the Jo Malone London AI Scent Advisor, an AI-powered experience that helps customers conversationally discover their perfect fragrance. Trained on Jo Malone London’s deep scent expertise and the “tell, explain, describe” framework utilized by in-store stylists, the tool employs an AI agent that helps customers to describe what they’re looking for, drawing out memories, sensory experiences, and their desired scent identity. Using Google’s Gemini models, the agent interprets these responses and maps them to the brand’s proprietary olfactive taxonomy, using a ranking engine built on Agent Platform to select the best fragrance candidates. The agent then crafts a personalized response, explaining why these scents are a match. Google Cloud — including Cloud Run, Pub/Sub, Firestore, BigQuery, and Model Armor — forms the backbone of the AI Scent Advisor, ensuring reliable, secure AI experiences at scale.
[...]
*Key takeaways and highlights from our interview with Chris Curro, Principal Machine Learning Engineer at the Estée Lauder Companies:*
[...]
→ “The way an expert speaks is not necessarily what an individual consumer brings to the table. That’s where a broader agentic system comes into play, powered by Google Gemini. We have an interactive agent to evoke the key points of inquiry to make a fragrance recommendation and really tell the consumer why is this the fragrance for them, based on what they’ve been able to describe.” 

→ “We built the Jo Malone London AI Scent Advisor on a wide variety of Google Cloud products. We’re using Google Gemini to power the actual core AI portion of the experience. Vertex AI pipelines for orchestrating the key data processing. We also store our analytics on BigQuery. We’re really taking all of this together to make a resilient, reliable, scalable solution.

results.title: How Estée Lauder Uses AI to Improve Factory Performance | ASD Market Week
highlights:
How Estée Lauder Uses AI to Improve Factory Performance | ASD Market Week

# How Estée Lauder Uses AI to Improve Factory Performance

Published: May 21, 2026ASD Newsroom

Robert - stock.adobe.com

### Key Takeaways:

- Estée Lauder uses the Ella AI platform to boost factory performance, driving production efficiency, tighter quality control and faster product runs.
- Digital twins, robotics and vision systems automate inspection, lower errors and streamline operations for improved supply reliability and reduced downtime.
- Automation and AI support consistent product quality, help retailers avoid out-of-stocks and enable fresher launches in stores.
- Focused technology upgrades like Ella help brands manage margins, optimize inventory and scale improvements across facilities.

### How Estée Lauder Uses Ella to Improve Factory Performance

Estée Lauder developed Ella, or “Estée Lauder Line Assistant,” to accelerate production and tighten quality control. The tool helps operators flag equipment issues sooner and speeds up switches between product runs, decreasing errors and costs. Ella is live in two facilities and will expand to more sites. It guides teams through diagnosing faults and recovering quickly, reduces trial runs for product switches and cuts down on wasted components and downtime. These improvements give retailers and customers more steady product availability and help brands align with seasonal demand while reducing inventory risks.

### How Do Vision Systems, Digital Twins, and Robotics Improve Quality and Efficiency?

Vision systems, digital twins, and robotics improve quality and efficiency by automating key factory processes. Vision cameras and software check items like vials, caps, and cartons much quicker and more precisely than manual inspection, catching even small defects and reducing rework. Digital twins let engineers test process changes in a virtual model, shortening design cycles and lowering the risk of last-minute fixes. These systems depend on clean, consistent data to represent real-world processes. In warehouses, mobile robots and drones cut repetitive work, speed inventory checks and make training easier, notably during peak times.

### What Is the Business Impact for Retailers and How Can Brands Succeed?

The business impact for retailers is more reliable production, which means fresher launches on shelves, fewer out-of-stocks and consistent product quality from batch to batch. This supports assortment planning, smooths seasonal resets and helps manage margins. For brands, success comes from focusing on use cases that benefit operators daily, building a foundation of standardized data and processes, and making sure teams know how and why tools are used. Scaling improvements takes clear standards, decision rights and tight system integration. The technology upgrades are practical, not flashy, but with the right approach, they add up to less waste, faster response and factories that keep surprises to a minimum.

(Note: AI assisted in summarizing the key points for this story.)

highlights:
L’Oréal, established in 1909 is partnering with NVIDIA, while 80-year-old Estée Lauder, is collaborating with Microsoft and OpenAI. Both expect to see significant makeovers of their opportunities for growth as a result of operating efficiencies, as well as a boost to their overall Customer Experience.
[...]
AI is truly going to power up beauty, to augment beauty in many ways. We embarked a few years ago now on AI and now generative AI. The R&D of L’Oréal was working with AI already a long time ago, but it's really a big acceleration now.
[...]
Probably the part where it is the most transformative is on the way people look for beauty. Now more than half of consumers, especially the younger ones, do not go through the search bar. They ask questions to Large Language Models (LLMs), and, of course, we want to make sure that we become the reference source of knowledge for all LLMs. So we have multiple partnerships with all the big AI companies to make sure that the
[...]
power allows consumers to find the best answers in their beauty quest. And of course, the best
[...]
Over at Estée Lauder, it’s a similar story according to Roberto Canevari, the firm’s Chief Value Chain Officer, where there have been significant leaps in AI-enabled productivity, doubling the number of launches that can be done in a year. This is happening for two reasons:
[...]
One risk that we wanted to avoid was to have a lot of AI initiatives, not connected, a lot of pilots one after the other, which are then difficult to scale. What we wanted to do since the start is to say that whatever we do has to have an end-to-end view first, a full value chain view, and second, we are able to scale it. So we have created internally what we call our Digital Atelier, which is basically a competence center where we identify and define how to scale the best digital solution, AI solution.
[...]
We are developing an intelligent technical packaging design using AI. What we're trying to do is to link a marketing brief to a packaging that is either already existing or similar packaging that are aligned with the brief. With small modifications, they are immediately industrialized, meaning it's much faster, [in terms of] the way we develop the packaging first.
[...]
Another example of AI in action comes in the shape of ELLA, not a new super-model, but the Estée Lauder Line Assistant, pitched by Canevari as “our new colleague”. He explains:
[...]
It's essentially an AI solution augmenting the capabilities of our people to identify immediately the best set-up of the line for a launch or for a change in the line for a performance requirement. Something that used to [take] quite a lot with different line operators is now instantaneously provided by ELLA. So again, it's speed, it's agility and it's efficiency because, of course, this is allowing more time to produce and more time to set up.
[...]
Another important aspect of the L’Oréal AI strategy centers on employees. A couple of years ago Hieronimus posited his thesis on how the rise of AI would impact on jobs. It was a sober analysis at a time when ‘AI is coming for all our jobs’ was the prevailing hysteria. He argued:
[...]
Short term, [AI is] a job creator. Half of the hirings we've been doing over the last three years has been either related to data or to AI. So right now it's creating jobs. Mid-term, I see my teams, they're all working too much and they're desperately hoping to have some sort of solution that helps them crunch the data, come up with better PowerPoints and not waste hours doing them. There may be some industries or some type of jobs where it's going to be a bit more radical, but I see this as a real way to free time and probably get our employees to have a better work life balance.
[...]
We've trained more than 65,000 L’Oréal-ans how to use AI with the L’Oréal GPT. They have a test that can help them assess their own mastery, and we provide more and more sophisticated learning.

results.title: Estée Lauder Already Knew Its Consumer. That Was Never the Problem. - RETHINK Retail
highlights:
“Relevance has a short shelf life.” That was Nadine Graf, President of Europe, UK&I and Emerging Markets at The Estée Lauder Companies, delivering the line that should make every legacy retailer in the room uncomfortable. Not because it’s wrong, but because it’s an indictment of the very model that built ELC into a global leader. For 80 years, the company pioneered prestige beauty. Then the clock changed, and the pioneering started to look like lead time.
[...]
Strip away the strategy language and one figure does all the work. In the old model, bringing a researched, tested, quality product to market took 18 to 24 months. Graf’s claim for the new one: AI compresses that process to as little as 72 hours.
[...]
Six months to seventy-two hours isn’t an efficiency gain. It’s a different physics. The point isn’t the technology; it’s what the technology admits. A two-year development cycle was survivable when beauty moved at the speed of seasonal launches. It’s fatal when a fragrance goes viral overnight and the demand evaporates before the supply chain has finished its first meeting. ELC isn’t adopting AI to look modern. It’s adopting AI because its own metabolism was the liability.
[...]
Graf promised to return in 18 months to report on Beauty Reimagined, ELC’s strategic plan to rewire how it grows, invests, and operates. The honest framing of what she’ll be graded on isn’t market share or margin. It’s whether a legacy giant can permanently reset its own clock speed: whether 72-hour development, edge-empowered markets, and amplify-the-moment instincts become the institution, or revert to the postmortem habit the moment the keynote lights go down.

results.title: The Estee Lauder Companies Shares New Research Findings at the 21st Annual Symposium for Cosmetic Advances & Laser Education (SCALE) Conference | MarketScreener
highlights:
NEW YORK, NY - The Estee Lauder Companies Inc. (NYSE: EL) announced that the company and several of its brands presented a range of novel findings at the 21st Annual Symposium for Cosmetic Advances & Laser Education (SCALE) Conference, held this month in Nashville. SCALE is the premier aesthetics and medical dermatology symposium, where leading aesthetic and medical dermatologists come together to share emerging science and clinical innovation.
[...]
'At The Estee Lauder Companies, our scientists continue to push the boundaries of skin and hair research, advancing scientific insights into areas such as glycation, cellular communication, UV protection, and sensitive skin biology,' said Claude Saliou, PhD, Senior Vice President, Advanced Technologies and Global Consumer and Clinical Sciences, The Estee Lauder Companies. 'The findings presented at SCALE demonstrate how our teams are combining clinical research with advanced technologies to deliver prestige skincare innovations backed by rigorous science.'
[...]
Research findings were presented on behalf of the company by select external partners and board-certified dermatologists, Dr. Jacqueline Watchmaker and Dr. Michael Gold, at Continuing Medical Education sessions at the conference. Dr. Jacqueline Watchmaker, MD, Center for Aesthetic and Laser Medicine Scottsdale, presented data on glycation and technologies to target their accumulation in the skin. Dr. Michael Gold, MD, Founder and Medical Director, Gold Skin Care Center, presented new findings from Clinique to support the new UV Solutions product franchise as well as La Mer's periorbital science tied to the latest Rejuvenating Eye Cream launch.
[...]
Estee Lauder hosted a symposium titled, 'The Biology of Night Repair: Circadian Epigenetic Regulation, microRNA Signaling, and Exosome-Mediated Cellular Communication.' The session was presented by Selma Masri, PhD, University of California, Irvine; Rishabh Kala, PhD, Lead Scientist, Estee Lauder; and Ted Lain, MD, MBA, DOCS Dermatology. Dr. Masri discussed the role of the circadian clock in health and disease and the importance of maintaining healthy biological rhythms as we age. In his presentation, Dr. Kala presented data on skin circadian rhythm and the role of miRNA signaling in comprehensive skin repair. Finally, Dr. Lain delivered new in vitro findings on the soothing benefits of a plant-derived exosome ingredient and the clinical results of an upcoming product launch in an existing franchise containing this technology.
[...]
Lisa DiNatale, Director, Clinical Testing (Skincare), La Mer Max Huber Research Laboratories, presented research findings on a 'Macrocystis pyrifera ferment-containing serum demonstrates efficacy in addressing rosacea, eczema, and extreme sensitive skin' detailing mechanistic and clinical evidence that the serum supports barrier repair, modulates neuroinflammatory signaling, and significantly improves erythema, dryness, itching, and tolerability in sensitive and hyperreactive skin. The serum was also confirmed to be appropriate for individuals with rosacea and eczema prone skin.
[...]
Around the world, ELC is dedicated to cutting-edge science with multidisciplinary expertise ranging from basic science and advanced technologies to the intersections of physics, chemistry, biology, and engineering. ELC R&I has 80 years of formulation authority and is deeply integrated into the scientific community, regularly presenting at leading events and publishing in peer-reviewed journals. ELC's R&D team consistently aims to develop the next generation of transformative, science-driven prestige beauty products, packaging, and experiences that deliver exceptional quality and performance for our consumers around the world.
